channel.title: eevy.in — Practical EV guidance for India
channel.description: Recent content on eevy.in — Practical EV guidance for India

item.title: Disclaimer
item.description:
eevy.India

Effective Date: 24 June 2026

1. General Information Only

The content on eevy.in — including readiness assessments, ownership summaries, and any associated reports — is provided for general informational purposes only. It does not constitute professional advice of any kind, including but not limited to legal, financial, technical, or vehicular advice.

2. Accuracy of Assessment Results

Readiness assessments are based on aggregated data and publicly available information about EV infrastructure, charging networks, and service availability in India. Results reflect conditions as understood at the time of generation. They may not account for:

item.title: Privacy Policy
item.description:
eevy.India

Effective Date: 24 June 2026

1. Who We Are

eevy.India (accessible at eevy.in) is an EV infrastructure and readiness assessment platform operated in India. We help prospective EV buyers understand readiness in their area, and we collect anonymised ownership experience data from existing EV owners to improve those assessments.

2. What Information We Collect

We collect two categories of information:

a) Readiness Assessment (prospective buyers)

  • Your approximate location (city / pin code)

item.title: Home Charging - What You Need to Know Before Buying in India
item.description:
Understanding the Type, Requirements and the Installation Basics

If I continue on this path of ultimately buying an EV for my daily use, one of the critical criteria today would be home charging. And since I live in a major city like Bangalore, and in an independent house, that decision becomes very easy. I have a 3 phase connection at home, the OEM will provide for a home charging unit and will also, most of the times, arrange for the installation.

item.title: Why Buying an EV in India Needs More Planning Than Most People Realize
item.description:
The Test Drive Is Probably The Last Thing You Need

Last night, while trying to research on the new e Vitara and Maruti’s Battery-as-a-Service (BaaS) offer, I stumbled upon the callback form and filled it up.

Today morning, promptly I received the call from the nearest Nexa showroom.

Like any regular buyer, I told them that I wanted information about the e Vitara and the BaaS offer. The caller tried explaining the details, but when I started probing a little deeper, she suggested that her manager would be able to answer my questions better and promised a callback in 10 minutes.

item.title: Is the EV Industry Solving the Wrong Problem with BaaS?
item.description:
The New Urban Cruiser Ebella -

Toyota offically launched its first battery-electric vehicle for the Indian market, on May 29th named - the Ebella. It is expected to be available in 3 different variants - Only the top-spec E3 variant is priced at ₹23.60 lakh (ex-showroom) was announced yesterday. Even the official website only shows the specs for the E3. I’m assuming there will be E2 and E1 naturally which will be revealed in the near-future. However, the website and the brochure show a BaaS version at ₹15.25 Lakhs + a Bettery EMI of ₹4.99 per Km. It isn’t very clear from the details if E3 is also being offered in the BaaS format or if BaaS is a variant on its own. The Ebella also comes with a 60% assured buy back and an eight year battery warranty. You can look up other details on their website or go through the number of YouTube videos around this car to learn more.

item.title: Why EV Range Anxiety Feels Familiar to Indian Drivers
item.description:
Back When Planning Was Part of Driving

It took a little more than 50 years after independence for the first ‘petrol bunk’ to open up at our village. Until then, every time my uncle hired a car to go somewhere, there was some serious amount of planning that used to go in before the trip - which route to take, where to eat, where to spend the night, and most importantly, where to refuel.

item.title: India's EV Market Is Entering The Free Charging Era
item.description:
The “Free Charging” Era — And Why That Changes Everything

Free charging offers are slowly becoming common in India’s EV space. But this shift may actually signal something much larger happening behind the scenes.

What’s happening in India’s EV market right now is actually very interesting.

A few years ago, EV companies were competing mostly on:

  • range,
  • battery size,
  • features,
  • and pricing.

Now?

They’re slowly starting to compete on something much bigger: the ownership experience.

item.title: Not All EVs Are Born Equal
item.description:
Born Electric vs ICE-Derived Platforms

I spent a major part of my childhood in Delhi, before relocating down south to Trichy, our hometown, due to dad’s transfers, of course. Delhi and Trichy used to have a common mode of transport during the 80’s - the rickshaw. No, I’m not not talking about the ‘Tuk-Tuk’, but the cycle rickshaw.

It was more or less a tricycle with some seating arrangements for the passengers. I’m sure this was common across India and maybe still exists in some corners. But it needed a lot of manual effort to be able to operate these. It was like riding a cycle, but with 2-3 passengers riding at the back, it must have been a tough ride.

item.title: Everyone Talks About EVs - These Are the EVs Actually Selling Today
item.description:
The real EVs that are making a killing in the market, very silently.

Last evening, I ordered pizza through Zomato. The delivery partner reached my door on time.

Now, since I have an entry control app and the guard at the gate also calls in to confirm, I have enough bells and whistles to know when a delivery person arrives. I also check from my window facing the road to see the moment he reaches my door, and I open the door to receive the items.

item.title: To EV or Not To EV
item.description:
A Thought That Started It All

Imagine this - The petrol car that you own is 3 years old, run for about 30k kms and recently completed its third free service for which you ended up paying ₹12k, and feel cheated. It is an underwhelming car, nothing really exciting in terms of performance, mileage, nothing. Life’s become boring and costly with this car.

That is my story right now. I bought a petrol car back in 2023, after returning from Europe. I did not do proper research and out of desperation and pressure bought a boring little 7 seater. Life has been slow ever since I drove the car out the showroom. Today (exactly 3 years since I bought the car), while I was renewing my insurance, I thought to myself - “Hey, why not go for a change.”

item.title: What Is Regenerative Braking?
item.description:
Free current anyone?

We were in Delhi at that time, and I was 11 when dad bought me and my brother our first cycles.

Mine was a simple kids’ cycle similar to my brother’s, but his was a little smaller in size (obviously) and a little more colorful too. So obviously, his looked a little jazzier than mine.

Dad had spent a little more so my brother would like the colours and grow an interest in riding, and to compensate, he got mine a headlight.

item.title: Contact
item.description:
Get in touch

If you have a question, suggestion, or want to collaborate, reach out.

Email

hello@eevy.in

What you can contact about

  • EV-related questions
  • Feedback on articles or tools
  • Collaboration or partnerships

I read every message, but replies may take some time.

item.title: Resources
item.description:
Tools and references to make better EV decisions

This page contains practical tools and structured guides to help you evaluate EVs in India using real numbers and real usage patterns.

1. EV Cost Reality (Simple Model)

Before buying an EV, this is the only calculation that matters:

Are you actually saving money?

Use this quick framework:

Step 1 — Monthly running

  • Daily usage (km): ___
  • Monthly km = daily × 30

Step 2 — Cost per km

Petrol:

item.title: About
item.description:
Clarity on EVs. Without the noise.

eevy.in exists for one reason: to help you make a clear, financially sensible decision about electric vehicles in India.

Right now, most EV content falls into two extremes — overly optimistic or unnecessarily technical. You either get marketing-driven hype, or spec-heavy breakdowns that don’t translate to real-world usage.

eevy cuts through that noise.

Every article is built around practical questions:

  • Does this EV actually save you money?
  • How does it perform in real Indian conditions?
  • What are the trade-offs nobody talks about?
  • Is it the right choice for your usage — not someone else’s?

No paid reviews. No brand bias. No unnecessary jargon.
